## Completed Wave 103

Wave 103: derived Wave 112, tailor narrative intelligence phase 9.

Rationale:

- Derived Wave 112 asked for narrative output that is generated or validated from evidence with uncertainty and repair guidance. The narrative tailor already produced proof-first, caveat-forward, and ask-ready variants, but its repair guidance was mostly free text and the overall score could imply the drafts were excellent even when they were not safe for external use. The compounding improvement was to add a structured manual readiness gate for every audience and calibrate the top-level score so blocked drafts do not masquerade as ready.

Changes:

- Added `manualReadinessGate` to every audience in `data/narrative-tailor.js`.
- Each gate now includes:
  - readiness status: `manual-review-ready`, `restricted-draft`, or `repair-before-use`
  - readiness score
  - blocker list with severity, details, evidence IDs, repair action, and verification command
  - review checklist
  - must-disclose items
  - ranked repair plan
  - evidence IDs
  - forbidden actions including `send-outreach`, `submit-application`, and `expose-private-material`
  - manual verification command
- Gate blockers are derived from:
  - high-risk narrative objections
  - low-grounding tailored variants
  - failing research stress scenarios
  - blocked opportunity-board lanes
  - private reference review needs
  - screenshot gaps
  - missing draft-only/manual boundary
- Fixed narrative tailoring lane lookup so it can use `opportunityBoard.audienceLanes` via `primaryPacketId`, audience text, and lane IDs instead of only a non-existent `lane.packages` shape.
- Added summary readiness fields:
  - `averageManualReadinessScore`
  - `manualReadinessReady`
  - `manualReadinessRestricted`
  - `manualReadinessBlocked`
  - `manualRepairPlanItems`
- Calibrated the top-level narrative tailor score to include manual readiness scores. This intentionally moved the final tailor report from a misleading high score to `77/100` because all three audience drafts are currently blocked before external use.
- Added `manual-readiness-gate` audience check and `manual-readiness-gates` report check.
- Extended narrative-tailor receipt payloads with readiness status, readiness score, blocker counts, and repair-plan counts.
- Updated terminal `narrative-tailor` output to show readiness counts, per-audience gate status, blocker counts, and top gate repair actions.
- Updated validator and API contract tests for readiness summary fields, gate shape, forbidden actions, repair plans, and audience checks.
- Preserved public-safety boundaries: tailored narratives remain deterministic local drafts; no outreach, applications, publishing, external services, private document exposure, or automatic sends were added.

Evidence:

- Syntax checks passed for:

  - `node --check test/api-contract.test.mjs`

## Completed Wave 104

Wave 104: derived Wave 113, summarize evidence graph depth phase 10.

Rationale:

- Derived Wave 113 asked for another verifiable graph-depth improvement. After Wave 103 added manual narrative readiness gates, the graph-depth report still treated narrative sequencing as a strong bridge without summarizing the fact that tailored narrative drafts were blocked before external use. The compounding improvement was to connect the evidence graph to the new manual narrative gates and summarize the path from narrative to tailored draft to blocker to repair.

Changes:

- Extended `data/graph-depth-score.js` to consume `narrativeTailor`.
- Added a new graph-depth lane: `manual-narrative-gate-depth`.
- The new lane scores and verifies:
  - audience gate coverage
  - average manual narrative readiness
  - blocked audience counts
  - repair-plan item counts
  - narrative tailor verification command coverage
- Added graph-depth summary fields:
  - `manualNarrativeReadinessScore`
  - `manualNarrativeReady`
  - `manualNarrativeRestricted`
  - `manualNarrativeBlocked`
  - `manualNarrativeRepairItems`
- Added a new depth-map path: `narrative-gate-to-repair`.
  - path shape: `narrative -> tailored draft -> manual readiness gate -> blocker -> repair`
  - depth: `7`
  - evidence count: manual repair-plan item count
- Added `depthSummaries`, with status, score, evidence count, linked lane, repair action, and verification command for every depth path.
- Added graph-depth checks:
  - `manual-narrative-gates`
  - `depth-summary-cards`
- Updated `currentGraphDepthScoreReport` in `server.js` to pass the current narrative tailor report.
- Updated terminal `graph-depth` output to show manual narrative readiness, ready/restricted/blocked counts, repair items, and depth summary cards.
- Updated the graph-depth receipt payload with `depthSummaries`.
- Updated validator fixtures/assertions and API contract tests for the manual narrative gate lane, summary fields, depth path, and summary-card checks.
- Preserved graph truth boundaries: blocked narrative gates are now visible in graph depth instead of being hidden behind high sequence scores.

## Completed Wave 105

Wave 105: derived Wave 114, replay artifact museum phase 10.

Rationale:

- Derived Wave 114 asked for a public-safe artifact type, screenshot, transcript, replay, or gap-record improvement. Artifact replays already exposed screenshot gaps, but those gaps were mostly absence records with a repair sentence. The compounding improvement was to make every screenshot gap a replayable public-safe closure plan artifact, so missing media is handled as an inspectable repair path instead of silently weakening the museum.

Changes:

- Added a new public-safe artifact type in `data/artifact-model.js`: `gap-closure-plan`.
- Each project now has one `gap-closure-plan` artifact with:
  - URL `/api/artifact-replays/:slug`
  - terminal command `artifact-replays <slug>`
  - source trace linking project record, gap record, API route, and terminal command
  - explicit policy that it is not a screenshot, video, private file, or proof that richer media exists
- Artifact catalog summary now includes `gapClosurePlans`.
- Extended artifact replay catalog in `data/artifact-replays.js`:
  - added a `gap-closure-plan` replay step
  - added `gapClosureSteps`
  - added ranked `gapClosurePlan` items with action, acceptance rule, fallback artifact, forbidden claim, and verification command
  - added summary counts `gapClosurePlans` and `gapClosureActions`
  - added `gap-closure-plan` replay check
  - calibrated replay scoring to reward closure-plan coverage without pretending screenshots exist
- Extended artifact museum audit in `data/artifact-museum.js`:
  - summary `gapClosurePlans`
  - `gap-closure-readiness` dimension
  - `gap-closure-plan-coverage` check
  - artifact replay routes included in inspection route coverage
- Updated terminal output:
  - `artifact-museum` now reports gap closure plan counts
  - `artifact-replays` now reports closure plan step counts
  - selected project replay output lists closure plan actions
- Updated validator and API contract tests for artifact counts, artifact type shape, museum summary/checks, replay summary, replay closure steps, closure plan shape, and forbidden screenshot/video/private-file claims.
- Preserved truth boundaries: no screenshots, videos, live recordings, or private files were claimed to exist; missing screenshots remain explicit repairable gaps.

## Blockers

- Full `maygoals.md` completion is intentionally impossible-scale and remains far from complete.
- Git metadata was repaired enough for local status, but no prior branch/ref history was recoverable from refs in this wave. Existing object data was not deleted.
- In-app Browser backend was unavailable for Wave 89 browser verification, so local Playwright was used as the browser fallback after the repo E2E suite passed.
- In-app Browser backend was also unavailable for Wave 93 rendered verification (`iab` unavailable), so local Playwright was used for the skip-link interaction proof.

## Completed Wave 106

Wave 106: derived Wave 115, schedule private chief of staff phase 10.

Rationale:

- The private chief-of-staff path already had lanes, local review boards, briefing drafts, and a flat local schedule. The next compounding improvement was to make scheduling safer and more operational: every chief lane now gets a private review window, stale-proof pressure is explicit, and external writes are blocked by a first-class local calendar firewall instead of implied prose.

Changes:

- Added private schedule route coverage:
  - `/api/private/schedule/plan`
  - `/api/private/schedule/history`
  - route manifest coverage in `data/runtime-attestation.js`
  - server handlers behind `ENABLE_PRIVATE_COCKPIT=1` and localhost-only private gating
- Extended `data/private-chief-of-staff.js` with `scheduleHandOff`:
  - one manual-only handoff per chief lane
  - recommended local review window
  - disabled calendar, reminder, and external writes
  - blocked external actions for calendar events, reminders, email, DMs, submissions, approvals, deployments, and payments
  - new `schedule-handoff` readiness check
- Extended `data/private-schedule.js` with:
  - `calendarFirewall`
  - `reviewWindows`
  - pressure scores and stale-proof pressure
  - source traces from schedule block to chief lane to review session
  - dependencies on local tasks, briefing drafts, approvals, and outreach drafts
  - explicit blocked actions per lane
  - new checks for review-window depth, calendar firewall, stale-proof pressure, and window source trace
- Updated private receipts:
  - `scripts/private-chief-of-staff.mjs` now records schedule handoff counts.
  - `scripts/private-schedule.mjs` now records calendar firewall and review window proof.
- Updated validators and API contracts for chief handoffs, schedule windows, firewall fields, blocked writes, route manifest entries, and receipt coverage.
- Preserved the privacy boundary: no calendars, inboxes, portals, emails, DMs, reminders, submissions, approvals, deployments, payments, or third-party systems are read or mutated.

## Completed Wave 107

Wave 107: derived Wave 116, de-risk opportunity engine phase 10.

Rationale:

- Opportunity de-risking already had risk scores, de-risk steps, manual gates, and receipts. The next compounding improvement was to make the hidden "external facts we do not know" impossible to miss. Every opportunity package now carries a public-safe assumption audit, a claim firewall, and a go/no-go gate so the app cannot quietly imply live postings, deadlines, application state, recipient interest, funding, or real-world upside.

Changes:

- Extended `data/opportunity-derisking.js` with per-package `assumptionAudit`:
  - live posting exists
  - deadline is known
  - application state is known
  - recipient interest exists
  - requirements are covered
  - high risks are cleared
  - effort and upside are calibrated
- Added an `opportunity-external-claim-firewall` to every de-risk plan:
  - blocks unverified live-posting, deadline, application-state, recipient-interest, requirement-fit, risk, effort, and upside claims
  - provides public-safe replacement language
  - keeps external writes disabled
  - forbids sending, submitting, scheduling, claiming recipient interest, and third-party writes
- Added `manualGoNoGo` gates with only three allowed states:
  - `manual-review-only`
  - `repair-first`
  - `internal-only`

## Completed Wave 108

Wave 108: derived Wave 117, explain runtime truth phase 10.

Rationale:

- Runtime explanation already translated local receipts into proof claims, but it still relied mostly on prose non-claims. The next compounding improvement was to make those limits operational: every runtime explanation now carries a claim firewall and an audit ladder, so local proof, blocked claims, receipt custody, and manual production-parity handoff are visible as structured data, receipts, terminal output, validators, and tests.

Changes:

- Extended `data/runtime-explain.js` with `runtime-truth-claim-firewall`:
  - allowed local proof claims derived from current proof claims
  - blocked claims for production deploy identity, CDN cache, external uptime, private cockpit contents, provider dashboards, and future drift
  - public-safe replacement language for each blocked claim
  - external writes disabled
- Added `runtime-truth-audit-ladder`:
  - runtime fingerprint
  - surface probes
  - public evidence refresh
  - receipt reconciliation
  - non-claim firewall
  - manual comparison handoff
- Added summary fields for receipt explanations, audit ladder steps, blocked runtime claims, and safe local claims.
- Added checks:
  - `receipt-explanation-custody`
  - `claim-firewall`
  - `audit-ladder`
- Updated `scripts/runtime-explain.mjs` receipts and console output to record blocked claim and audit ladder counts.
- Updated terminal `runtime-explain` output to show firewall and blocked-claim details.
- Updated validator and API contract tests for the claim firewall, audit ladder, custody checks, blocked claims, and summary fields.
- Preserved runtime truth boundaries: no deploy, publish, git mutation, private-route enablement, provider dashboard query, CDN check, DNS check, private cockpit read, or third-party contact.

Evidence:

- Syntax checks passed for changed files:
  - `data/runtime-explain.js`
  - `scripts/runtime-explain.mjs`
  - `server.js`
  - `scripts/validate-data.mjs`
  - `test/api-contract.test.mjs`
- `node scripts/validate-data.mjs` passed: `Validated 13 projects, 74 claims, 103 artifacts, 13 checks, 41 private references.`
- Runtime receipts:
  - `npm run explain:runtime` produced `runtime-explain-mpp50ikz 9/9 check(s) passed; 6 proof claim(s); 6 blocked claim(s); 6 audit step(s); score 100/100; wrote var/runtime-explain-receipts.json`

## Completed Wave 109

Wave 109: derived Wave 118, stabilize design ambition phase 10.

Rationale:

- Design stability and ambition were already measurable, but dense command controls still depended on scattered CSS/source checks. The next compounding improvement was to create one explicit control stability matrix that agents must keep passing before claiming the command center is ambitious, keyboard-safe, and mobile-safe.

Changes:

- Extended `data/design-stability.js` with a first-class `controlStabilityMatrix` covering:
  - proof ribbon action buttons
  - terminal shortcut grid
  - command jump navigation
  - search and terminal forms
  - artifact filter controls
  - skip-link rail
  - global focus ring
- Added design-stability summary fields for matrix item count, passing surfaces, responsive fallbacks, and keyboard-safe surfaces.
- Added the `control-stability-matrix` check so the report fails if dense controls lose stable geometry, responsive fallback coverage, keyboard safety, or command-backed verification.
- Extended `server.js` design source extraction to read all matching mobile media blocks, not just the first one, so later form/artifact fallbacks are audited accurately.
- Added mobile/form/artifact/skip-link source signals to the design extractor.
- Extended `data/design-ambition.js` with:
  - stability matrix summary fields
  - the `stability-matrix` control family
  - the `stability-matrix-visible` check
  - a stricter control-family depth requirement of at least 6 passing families
- Updated `scripts/design-stability.mjs` and `scripts/design-ambition.mjs` receipts and console output to preserve matrix evidence.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` fixtures/assertions and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` API assertions for the matrix, family, summary fields, and checks.
- Preserved public-safety boundaries: no analytics, private cockpit enablement, deploy, provider check, visitor data, external write, or third-party contact.

Evidence:

- Narrow checks:
  - `node --check server.js` passed.
  - `node --check data/design-ambition.js` passed.
  - `node scripts/validate-data.mjs` passed: `Validated 13 projects, 74 claims, 103 artifacts, 13 checks, 41 private references.`
- Design receipts:
  - First live matrix attempt correctly failed the new gate: `design-stability-mpp5itsn 9/10 check(s) passed; 5/7 stable surface(s); 29 shortcut(s); score 88/100`

## Completed Wave 110

Wave 110: derived Wave 119, grade research-grade evaluation phase 10.

Rationale:

- Research rigor had an aggregate score, dimensions, and checks, but the aggregate could hide which evaluator was weak. The next compounding improvement was to make the evaluation stack grade itself component-by-component, with floors, letter grades, evidence checklists, failure modes, repair actions, and verification commands.

Changes:

- Extended `data/research-rigor.js` with `evaluationGradebook` covering:
  - proof quality
  - search quality
  - opportunity quality
  - usability quality
  - design ambition
  - runtime evidence chain
  - research stress
  - evaluation integrity
- Added a `gradingRubric` with letter bands and a pass policy that prevents aggregate score from hiding a failing evaluator.
- Added research-rigor summary fields:
  - `gradebookItems`
  - `passingGradebookItems`
  - `failingGradebookItems`
  - `minimumGrade`
  - `averageGradeScore`
- Added the `gradebook-calibration` rigor dimension.
- Added the `gradebook-floor-coverage` check.
- Added `researchContract.gradingRule`.
- Updated `scripts/research-rigor.mjs` receipts and console output to preserve gradebook score/floor/grade/pass evidence.
- Updated terminal `research-rigor` output to show the gradebook summary and each evaluator grade.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` fixtures/assertions and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` API assertions for the gradebook, grading rubric, new dimension, new check, and summary fields.
- Preserved public-safety boundaries: no live user research, peer review claim, analytics collection, private cockpit read, deploy, publish, provider check, external service call, or third-party mutation.

## Completed Wave 111

Wave 111: derived Wave 120, fortify runtime truth phase 10.

Rationale:

- Runtime evidence chain was coherent locally, but a coherent local chain can still invite overclaiming about production, CDN, provider state, hosted routes, and private gates. The next compounding improvement was to add hosted-parity tripwires: local runtime truth can pass while still explicitly blocking production-parity claims until hosted public runtime truth is manually read.

Changes:

- Extended `data/runtime-evidence-chain.js` with `parityTripwires` covering:
  - hosted identity parity
  - provider deploy state
  - CDN/DNS/external uptime parity
  - hosted route-surface parity
  - private-route production gate parity
  - human explanation as production proof
- Added `custodyPacket.parityTripwireRule`.
- Added runtime chain summary fields:
  - `parityTripwires`
  - `blockedParityClaims`
  - `manualParityChecks`
- Added the `production-parity-tripwires` check.
- Updated `scripts/runtime-evidence-chain.mjs` receipts and console output to preserve tripwire evidence.
- Updated terminal `runtime-chain` output to show hosted-parity tripwire counts and blocked claim replacements.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for tripwire count, blocked status, manual-read requirements, forbidden automation, custody rule, and check coverage.
- Preserved public-safety boundaries: no deploy, publish, git mutation, private cockpit read, provider dashboard query, CDN inspection, DNS check, uptime probe, external service call, or third-party mutation.

- Narrow checks:

  - `node --check server.js` passed.

## Completed Wave 112

Wave 112: derived Wave 121, normalize evidence graph phase 10.

Rationale:

- The graph scoreboard counted modeled entities and coverage, but it did not expose a durable normalization ledger for every modeled family. The next compounding improvement was to make graph normalization explicit: each entity family now carries a canonical ID rule, public-safe policy, coverage status, missing count, promotion policy, and verification command.

Changes:

- Extended `data/graph-scoreboard.js` with `normalizationLedger`.
- Added canonical ID rules for graph families including person, project, claim, artifact, screenshot, repository, demo, opportunity, weakness, open task, verification receipt, narrative, narrative objection, audience packet, domain, system, and course.

  - `failing`

  - `repair-actions-commanded`
  - `quarantine-policy-visible`
- Added `methodology.canonicalIdRule`.
- Updated terminal `graph-scoreboard` output to show ledger coverage, checks, thin families, and sample canonical rules.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for the ledger, canonical rules, public-safe status, checks, and verification commands.
- Preserved public-safety boundaries: no invented collaborators, awards, publications, patents, private documents, external uptime, private runtime access, production graph persistence, analytics, deploy, publish, or third-party contact.

## Completed Wave 114

Wave 114: derived Wave 123, harden private cockpit phase 10.

Rationale:

- Private priorities were already manual-only at the item level, but the priority stack did not have a first-class execution firewall proving that every ranked action is locked away from external writes. The next hardening layer was to make those locks explicit, countable, receipt-backed, and embedded in the private cockpit payload.

Changes:

- Extended `data/private-priorities.js` with `local-private-priority-execution-firewall`.
- Added one execution lock per private priority item, including:
  - local-only/manual-only flags
  - human-only handoff status
  - forbidden external actions
  - replacement local action
  - local verification command
  - source trace
  - `locked-until-human-review` status
- Added private prioritization summary fields:
  - `executionLocks`
  - `manualOnlyExecutionLocks`
  - `blockedExternalActionSlots`
  - `replacementLocalActions`
- Added prioritization checks:
  - `execution-firewall-depth`
  - `execution-lock-manual-only`
  - `blocked-external-action-slots`
  - `replacement-local-actions`
- Updated `scripts/private-priorities.mjs` receipts and console output to preserve execution firewall evidence.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` to assert lock count parity, manual-only coverage, replacement local actions, blocked third-party mutation, and broad external-write blocking.
- Preserved private safety boundaries: no email, DM, calendar event, reminder, application submission, publication approval, production deploy, payment, portal automation, third-party mutation, or external outcome claim is performed by the app.

## Completed Wave 115

Wave 115: derived Wave 124, harden private task tracker phase 10.

Rationale:

- The private task tracker already stored status locally, but its POST mutation path did not have an explicit mutation policy, receipt script, or plan/history route. The next hardening layer was to make local task changes auditable while preserving the local-only/private route boundary.

Changes:

- Extended `data/private-task-tracker.js` with:
  - `local-private-task-tracker-plan`
  - `local-private-task-mutation-guard`
  - local-only/manual-only task flags
  - explicit status transition buckets
  - mutation locks for every private task
  - blocked external task actions
  - replacement local actions
  - local verification commands
  - local receipt read/write helpers
  - status normalization for the local store
- Added `scripts/private-task-tracker.mjs` and `npm run tasks:private`, which starts a temporary private-enabled localhost server, reads `/api/private/tasks`, and writes receipts to `var/private-task-receipts.json`.
- Added `/api/private/tasks/plan` and `/api/private/tasks/history`, both behind `ENABLE_PRIVATE_COCKPIT=1` and localhost-only private gating.
- Added the task plan/history routes to `runtimeRouteManifest`.
- Added `scripts/private-task-tracker.mjs` to `npm run check`.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for task mutation locks, blocked external actions, plan/history routes, package command coverage, and private gate behavior.
- Preserved safety boundaries: task mutations remain local JSON status bookkeeping only; no task sync, reminder, email, DM, calendar event, application submission, publication approval, deployment, payment, portal automation, third-party mutation, or external outcome claim is performed.

## Completed Wave 116

Wave 116: derived Wave 125, harden private review sessions phase 10.

Rationale:

- Private review sessions already described local review windows, but they did not have explicit decision gates, plan/history routes, or local receipts. The next hardening layer was to make every review session produce only local review states, with countable gates and blocked external actions.

Changes:

- Extended `data/private-review-sessions.js` with:
  - `local-private-review-sessions-plan`
  - `local-private-review-decision-gates`
  - one decision gate per review session
  - allowed local decisions
  - blocked external actions
  - replacement local actions
  - local verification commands
  - local receipt read/write helpers
  - score/check/repair-action summary fields
- Added `scripts/private-review-sessions.mjs` and `npm run review:private`, which starts a temporary private-enabled localhost server, reads `/api/private/review-sessions`, and writes receipts to `var/private-review-session-receipts.json`.
- Added `/api/private/review-sessions/plan` and `/api/private/review-sessions/history`, both behind `ENABLE_PRIVATE_COCKPIT=1` and localhost-only private gating.
- Added the review-session plan/history routes to `runtimeRouteManifest`.
- Added `scripts/private-review-sessions.mjs` to `npm run check`.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for review decision gates, route manifest coverage, package command coverage, private gate behavior, and blocked external actions.
- Fixed a test variable shadowing bug where the public-route 404 smoke variable hid the imported `privateReviewSessionsPlan` function.
- Preserved safety boundaries: review sessions remain local guidance only; no calendar event, reminder, email, DM, application submission, publication approval, deployment, payment, portal automation, task sync, third-party mutation, or external outcome claim is performed.

## Completed Wave 117

Wave 117: derived Wave 126, harden private briefing drafts phase 10.

Rationale:

- The lower-level private briefing drafts contained useful local planning briefs, but they did not have their own receipt script, plan/history routes, or explicit decision gates. The next hardening layer was to make every brief review-only and promotable only into local chief-draft review.

Changes:

- Extended `data/private-briefing-drafts.js` with:
  - `local-private-briefing-drafts-plan`
  - `local-private-briefing-decision-gates`

  - allowed local decisions
  - blocked external actions
  - replacement local actions
  - local verification commands
  - local receipt read/write helpers
  - score/check/repair-action summary fields

- Preserved `/api/private/briefing-drafts/:id` selection route by placing plan/history before the selection handler.

- Added `scripts/private-briefing-drafts.mjs` to `npm run check`.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for briefing gates, route manifest coverage, package command coverage, private gate behavior, selected draft route behavior, and blocked external actions.
- Preserved safety boundaries: briefing drafts remain review-only local planning artifacts; no email, DM, calendar event, reminder, application submission, publication approval, deployment, payment, portal automation, task sync, third-party mutation, or external outcome claim is performed.

## Completed Wave 118

Wave 118: derived Wave 127, harden outreach draft review phase 10.

Rationale:

- Outreach drafts are the highest-risk private planning surface because draft text can drift into accidental send/submit automation. The next hardening layer was to make every outreach draft explicitly unsendable, receipt-backed, and locked behind local send-prevention gates.

Changes:

- Extended `data/outreach-drafts.js` with:
  - `local-private-outreach-drafts-plan`
  - `local-private-outreach-send-prevention-gates`
  - one send-prevention gate per draft
  - disabled send/submit capabilities
  - blocked external actions
  - replacement local actions
  - local verification commands
  - local receipt read/write helpers
  - local audit entries that explicitly mark status updates as non-external writes
  - score/check/repair-action summary fields
- Added `scripts/private-outreach-drafts.mjs` and `npm run outreach:private`, which starts a temporary private-enabled localhost server, reads `/api/private/outreach-drafts`, and writes receipts to `var/outreach-draft-receipts.json`.
- Added `/api/private/outreach-drafts/plan` and `/api/private/outreach-drafts/history`, both behind `ENABLE_PRIVATE_COCKPIT=1` and localhost-only private gating.
- Added the outreach plan/history routes to `runtimeRouteManifest`.
- Added `scripts/private-outreach-drafts.mjs` to `npm run check`.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for send-prevention gates, route manifest coverage, package command coverage, private gate behavior, send/submit disabled state, zero sendable drafts, and blocked external actions.
- Preserved safety boundaries: outreach drafts remain local draft review artifacts; no email, DM, calendar event, reminder, application submission, form submission, publication approval, deployment, payment, portal automation, task sync, third-party mutation, or external outreach outcome claim is performed.

## Completed Wave 119

Wave 119: derived Wave 128, harden privacy approval phase 10.

Rationale:

- Privacy approvals are the upstream private boundary for claims and artifacts. The hardening pass made every approval candidate explicitly gated, local-only, receipt-backed, and unable to publish or mutate external systems from inside the app.

Changes:

- Extended `data/privacy-approval.js` with:
  - `local-private-approval-plan`
  - `local-private-approval-decision-gates`
  - one approval decision gate per private claim/artifact candidate
  - disabled external writes and disabled public projection writes
  - blocked external actions for publication, outreach, calendar writes, task sync, portal automation, deployment, payments, and third-party mutation
  - replacement local actions and local verification commands
  - local receipt read/write helpers
  - local audit entries that explicitly mark approval decisions as non-external writes
  - score/check/repair-action summary fields
- Added `scripts/private-approval.mjs` and `npm run approve:private`, which starts a temporary private-enabled localhost server, reads `/api/private/approvals`, and writes receipts to `var/private-approval-receipts.json`.
- Added `/api/private/approvals/plan` and `/api/private/approvals/history`, both behind `ENABLE_PRIVATE_COCKPIT=1` and localhost-only private gating.
- Added approval plan/history routes to `runtimeRouteManifest`.
- Added `scripts/private-approval.mjs` to `npm run check`.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for approval decision gates, route manifest coverage, package command coverage, private gate behavior, local receipt surfacing, blocked publication/private-material exposure, and external-write disabled state.
- Preserved safety boundaries: approval records remain local/private reviewer decisions; no private material is published, no email/DM/application/form/calendar/reminder/deploy/payment/portal/task-sync/third-party mutation occurs.

- Narrow checks:

## Completed Wave 120

Wave 120: harden private next-action planning phase 10.

Rationale:

- The private next-action planner is the root feed for tasks, reviews, briefing drafts, schedules, priorities, and chief-of-staff drafts. It had a private route but no dedicated recorder, receipt history, or explicit execution locks, so generated actions could look actionable without a first-class proof that the app itself cannot execute them.

Changes:

- Extended `data/private-planner.js` with:
  - `local-private-next-action-plan-recorder`
  - `local-private-next-action-execution-locks`
  - one execution lock per ranked private next action
  - disabled task execution and external writes
  - blocked external actions for outreach, submissions, calendar writes, task sync, deployment, payments, portal automation, publication approval, and third-party mutation
  - replacement local actions and local verification commands
  - local receipt read/write helpers
  - score/check/repair-action summary fields
- Added `scripts/private-next-actions.mjs` and `npm run plan:private`, which starts a temporary private-enabled localhost server, reads `/api/private/next-actions`, and writes receipts to `var/private-next-action-receipts.json`.
- Added `/api/private/next-actions/plan` and `/api/private/next-actions/history`, both behind `ENABLE_PRIVATE_COCKPIT=1` and localhost-only private gating.
- Added next-action plan/history routes to `runtimeRouteManifest`.
- Added `scripts/private-next-actions.mjs` to `npm run check`.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for next-action execution locks, route manifest coverage, package command coverage, private gate behavior, local receipt surfacing, planning-only action flags, and blocked external actions.
- Preserved safety boundaries: next actions remain local planning objects; no task execution, email, DM, application, form, calendar event, reminder, publication approval, deployment, payment, portal automation, task sync, or third-party mutation occurs.

## Completed Wave 121

Wave 121: harden private cockpit envelope phase 10.

Rationale:

- The private cockpit is the top-level aggregate local surface for the private intelligence machine. Its child systems had become receipt-backed and locked down, but the cockpit envelope itself still lacked a recorder, history route, surface export firewall, and route/script proof.

Changes:

- Extended `data/private-cockpit.js` with:
  - `local-private-cockpit-plan`
  - `local-private-cockpit-surface-firewall`
  - one local-only surface lock for each cockpit base section and embedded private report
  - disabled external writes, public exports, and downloads
  - blocked external actions for private cockpit export/download, private material publication, outreach, submissions, calendar writes, deployment, payments, task sync, portal automation, publication approval, and third-party mutation
  - embedded private report summaries for the full cockpit route
  - replacement local actions and local verification commands
  - local receipt read/write helpers
  - score/check/repair-action summary fields
- Added `scripts/private-cockpit.mjs` and `npm run cockpit:private`, which starts a temporary private-enabled localhost server, reads `/api/private/cockpit`, and writes receipts to `var/private-cockpit-receipts.json`.
- Added `/api/private/cockpit/plan` and `/api/private/cockpit/history`, both behind `ENABLE_PRIVATE_COCKPIT=1` and localhost-only private gating.
- Added a `currentPrivateCockpit()` server assembly path so the full cockpit route can build embedded report summaries without exposing or executing anything.
- Added cockpit plan/history routes to `runtimeRouteManifest`.
- Added `scripts/private-cockpit.mjs` to `npm run check`.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for cockpit surface locks, route manifest coverage, package command coverage, private gate behavior, local receipt surfacing, embedded report coverage, export/download disabled state, and blocked external actions.
- Preserved safety boundaries: the cockpit remains a local inspection surface; no private export/download, publication, email, DM, application, form, calendar event, reminder, publication approval, deployment, payment, portal automation, task sync, or third-party mutation occurs.

## Completed Wave 122

Wave 122: harden proof-trial receipts and sandbox firewall.

Rationale:

- Proof trials sit directly on the boundary between live evidence and unsafe automation. The catalog already described deterministic read-only replays, but it lacked a recorder, receipt history, plan route, and explicit sandbox firewall proving credentials, private documents, and external writes remain impossible.

Changes:

- Extended `data/proof-trials.js` with:
  - `safe-live-proof-trials-plan`
  - `read-only-proof-trial-sandbox-firewall`
  - one read-only sandbox lock per project proof trial
  - disabled credentials, external writes, and production mutation
  - blocked external actions for credential use, private document loading, outreach, submissions, calendar writes, deployments, payments, task sync, portal automation, publication approval, and third-party mutation
  - local receipt read/write helpers
  - score/check/repair-action summary fields
- Added `scripts/proof-trials.mjs` and `npm run trial:proofs`, which starts a temporary localhost server, reads `/api/proof-trials`, samples deterministic per-project trial results, and writes receipts to `var/proof-trial-receipts.json`.
- Added `/api/proof-trials/plan` and `/api/proof-trials/history`.
- Added proof-trial plan/history routes to `runtimeRouteManifest`.
- Added `scripts/proof-trials.mjs` to `npm run check`.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for proof-trial receipts, route manifest coverage, package command coverage, sandbox locks, read-only execution, credential bans, blocked external actions, and plan/history routes.
- Preserved safety boundaries: proof trials remain deterministic local read-only descriptors and assertions; no credentials, private documents, production writes, submissions, messages, payments, deployments, approvals, calendar writes, portal automation, or third-party mutation occur.

## Completed Wave 123

Wave 123: add receipt-backed proof-quality evaluation custody.

Rationale:

- Proof quality is the central score for whether the modeled evidence system is actually trustworthy. Before this wave it was a live calculation only; now it has an audit command, plan/history routes, receipt persistence, and explicit route/script custody checks.

Changes:

- Extended `data/proof-quality.js` with:
  - `research-grade-proof-quality-plan`
  - local receipt read/write helpers

  - proof-quality receipt summaries while preserving the original proof-quality score semantics

- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for proof-quality plan/history routes, receipt surfacing, audit coverage, package script coverage, and route manifest coverage.
- Preserved safety boundaries: proof-quality audits remain public-safe local scoring; no claims are changed, no private material is published, no third parties are contacted, and no credentials, deploys, payments, submissions, or external mutations occur.

## Completed Wave 124

Wave 124: add receipt-backed search-quality evaluation custody and repair proof-search relevance.

Rationale:

- Search quality is the front door for proof discovery. The app could grade search locally, but it had no recorder, history, route/script custody, or evidence that broad visitor questions actually surface the projects the proof graph says they should surface.
- The first recorder run exposed a real issue: all cases passed by score, but only `3/7` benchmark cases surfaced an expected proof project in the top three results. I fixed the search relevance path instead of weakening the audit.

Changes:

- Extended `data/search-quality.js` with:
  - `proof-backed-search-quality-plan`
  - local receipt read/write helpers
  - audit checks for benchmark depth, passing cases, expected-hit coverage, claim-link coverage, inspection-path coverage, analytics boundary, public route manifest coverage, and package script coverage
  - repair actions and receipt-aware summary fields
- Added `scripts/search-quality.mjs` and `npm run audit:search-quality`, which starts a temporary localhost server, reads `/api/evaluation/search-quality`, and writes receipts to `var/search-quality-receipts.json`.
- Added `/api/evaluation/search-quality/plan` and `/api/evaluation/search-quality/history`.
- Added search-quality plan/history routes to `runtimeRouteManifest`.
- Added `scripts/search-quality.mjs` to `npm run check`.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for search-quality plan/history routes, latest receipt surfacing, audit coverage, package script coverage, and route manifest coverage.
- Improved `rankProjects` in `server.js` with evidence-aware query expansion and domain ranking for proof-search intents such as agent infrastructure, production maturity, live demos, Bluetooth work, professor/research paths, ship proof, and stale/source repair. This keeps search local and deterministic while making broad proof questions discoverable.
- Preserved safety boundaries: search-quality audits use local public-safe fixtures and localhost requests only; no visitor analytics, private queries, remote embeddings, private documents, third-party accounts, credentials, or external writes are collected or mutated.

Evidence:

- Narrow checks:
  - `node --check data/search-quality.js` passed.
  - `node --check scripts/search-quality.mjs` passed.

## Completed Wave 125

Wave 125: add receipt-backed graph-quality custody.

Rationale:

- Graph quality is the structural safety net for the evidence graph: duplicate nodes, dangling edges, missing claim/project links, missing artifacts, and unsafe local text all funnel through it. The report already existed, but it lacked the recorder/history/plan custody pattern used by newer graph surfaces.

Changes:

- Extended `data/graph-quality.js` with:
  - `evidence-graph-quality-plan`
  - local receipt read/write helpers
  - audit summary fields for coverage score and latest receipt ID
  - repair actions and verification commands for every check
  - public route manifest and package script custody checks

- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` assertions for graph-quality plan/history routes, receipt surfacing, route/script custody, audit coverage, and verification commands.
- Preserved safety boundaries: graph-quality audits remain public-safe local graph checks; no private files are crawled, no credentials are loaded, no graph changes are published, no third parties are contacted, and no external systems are mutated.

## Completed Wave 127

Wave 127: add receipt-backed graph-scoreboard custody.

Rationale:

- Graph scoreboard normalizes the public evidence graph into canonical entity families, crosslink coverage, quarantine pressure, and repair actions. It already exposed useful weakness signals, but lacked recorder/history custody and route/script audit coverage.
- The new recorder deliberately preserves the honest substantive score: the custody checks pass, while the scoreboard still reports `63/100 low` graph normalization health. That low score is now visible, receipt-backed debt rather than hidden debt.

## Next Action

Wave 128: move from custody to substance by raising graph-scoreboard normalization health. Start with the largest score drains: rendered crosslink coverage, canonical entity coverage, and quarantine pressure, while preserving public-safe graph projection rules and full verification.

## Completed Wave 128

Wave 128: materially raise graph-scoreboard normalization health.

Rationale:

- Wave 127 made graph-scoreboard receipt-backed, but also exposed the real weakness: `63/100 low` normalization health, only `158/442` modeled entity references rendered, and only `206/408` crosslinks visible in the rendered graph.
- This wave upgrades the actual graph payload instead of merely changing the score. The graph now renders the public-safe endpoints the scoreboard already modeled: all claim nodes, all artifact nodes, canonical skill nodes, screenshot-gap nodes, repository proof nodes, demo proof nodes, award/hackathon signal nodes, timeline event nodes, and the full maintenance issue set.

Changes:

- Expanded `graphPayload()` in `server.js`:
  - skill nodes now include every skill family from `currentSkillGapMap()`, not only the first few project tags
  - claim nodes now include the full public claim projection, not only the top 16
  - artifact nodes now include the full artifact catalog, not only the top 18
  - maintenance nodes now include the full maintenance issue set
  - added explicit public-safe node families for screenshot gaps, repository proof, demo proof, award/hackathon signals, and timeline events
  - added graph edges from projects to screenshot gaps, repositories, demos, awards, and timeline events
  - added representation edges from repository/demo nodes to their concrete artifact records
- Updated `data/graph-scoreboard.js` repair-action deduplication so distinct repair reasons remain visible even when they share the same generic action text.
- Refreshed graph-quality and graph-scoreboard receipts after the larger graph payload.
- Preserved safety boundaries: new nodes are public-safe projections from existing local data. Screenshot nodes remain explicit gaps, repository/demo nodes point to existing artifact records, award nodes use local outcome/proof text, and timeline nodes use existing project timeline fields. No private artifacts are published and no external systems are touched.

  - `graph-scoreboard-current` from Wave 127: `63:low`, `158/442` rendered entity references, `206/408` rendered crosslinks, `7/7` custody checks.
- After graph expansion:
  - Live inspection showed graph quality at `442` nodes and `529` edges with `11/11` checks.
  - Live inspection showed graph-scoreboard at `86/100 high`, `442/442` rendered entity references, `408/408` rendered crosslinks, `7/7` checks, and `7` repair actions.
  - Dimensions now report:
    - `canonical-entity-coverage:100`

## Next Action

Wave 129: continue the substantive graph work by reducing or better governing graph quarantine pressure. Focus on distinguishing public-safe visible pressure-test objections from true graph quarantine, while keeping private references, weak evidence, and high-risk objections explicit and verifiable.

## Completed Wave 129

Wave 129: convert graph quarantine pressure into governed visible risk instead of fake all-clear or hard quarantine.

Rationale:

- After Wave 128, graph-scoreboard reached `86/100 high`, but quarantine pressure still scored `0/100` because every visible pressure-test objection and every private-reference claim projection was treated like blocking graph quarantine.
- The right fix was not to hide the risks. This wave makes the distinction explicit:
  - structural graph failures and contradictions remain blocking quarantine
  - public-safe private-reference claim projections are visible governed risk
  - rendered skeptical-reader objections are visible pressure tests
  - low-confidence relationships remain inspection-only links

Changes:

- Updated `data/graph-scoreboard.js` quarantine modeling:
  - quarantine items now carry `disposition` and `blocking`
  - summary now exposes `blocking`, `governed`, `visiblePressureTests`, `visibleGovernedRisks`, and `inspectionOnlyLinks`
  - quarantine scoring now penalizes true blocking quarantine strongly while applying lighter governance pressure to visible pressure tests and governed risks
  - quarantine-pressure dimension detail now reports blocking vs visible/governed risk instead of only total/high severity
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` to lock in the new governance contract:
  - disposition counts must reconcile to total
  - every quarantine item must expose `blocking` and `disposition`
  - graph-scoreboard must stay high scoring while preserving visible pressure-test risk
- Preserved safety boundaries: high-risk objections and private-reference claims remain present in the report. This wave does not claim those risks are solved; it classifies them accurately so they do not masquerade as hidden graph corruption.

Evidence:

- Before:
  - Wave 128 graph-scoreboard was `86/100 high`.
  - Quarantine-pressure dimension was still `0/100`.
  - Quarantine summary reported `31` candidates and `8` high severity without distinguishing blocking graph quarantine from visible governed risk.
- After:
  - Live inspection showed graph-scoreboard `97/100 high`.
  - Quarantine summary showed:
    - `31` total candidates
    - `0` blocking candidates
    - `31` governed candidates
    - `11` visible pressure tests
    - `12` visible governed risks
    - `8` inspection-only links
  - Quarantine-pressure dimension now scores `80/100` with detail `31 quarantine candidate(s), 0 blocking, 11 visible pressure-test, 12 governed risk.`
- Receipt and smoke:
  - `npm run audit:graph-scoreboard` produced `graph-scoreboard-mppd6wog 7/7 check(s) passed; score 97/100 high; 21 type(s); audit 100/100; wrote var/graph-scoreboard-receipts.json`

    - `graph-scoreboard-history:graph-scoreboard-mppd6wog`
    - `graph-scoreboard-quarantine-dim:80:31 quarantine candidate(s), 0 blocking, 11 visible pressure-test, 12 governed risk.`
- Runtime receipts:
  - `npm run record:runtime-surface` produced `surface-mppd82xs 212/212 passed; 33/33 private gate probe(s); wrote var/runtime-surface-receipts.json`
  - `npm run record:runtime` produced `runtime-mppd84go readiness 93/100; 3 runtime change(s); wrote var/runtime-truth-receipts.json`

## Completed Wave 130

Wave 130: make graph-lineage receipt-backed and contract-covered.

Rationale:

- `graph-lineage` had strong substantive coverage after the graph expansion, but it still lacked the custody layer now expected for major public-safe evaluation surfaces.
- The fix was to make objection lineage auditable end to end: a declared plan, package script, report/history routes, receipt persistence, runtime route coverage, API contract assertions, and full verification.

Changes:

- Upgraded `data/graph-lineage.js` to expose `graphLineagePlan()`, receipt read/write/append helpers, route/package manifest checks, `auditCoverageScore`, `latestReceiptId`, and repair actions for failed custody checks.

  - `/api/graph-lineage`

- Added graph-lineage plan/history to `data/runtime-attestation.js`.

- Expanded `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` so lineage custody is locked by local validation and live API tests.

## Next Action

## Completed Wave 131

Wave 131: promote tailored narrative projections into the public graph while preserving draft-only quarantine.

Rationale:

- `graph-projection-guard` already had plan/history/receipt custody, but its latest receipt exposed `81` quarantined projection relationships because tailored-narrative nodes were not rendered in `/api/graph`.
- The right fix was not to silence quarantine. This wave renders the valid tailored narrative graph structure and keeps low-confidence draft-only variants quarantined as manual-review gates.

Changes:

- Expanded `server.js` graph payload:
  - added `9` `tailored-narrative` nodes
  - added `81` tailored projection edges:
    - narrative -> tailored variant
    - tailored variant -> claim
    - tailored variant -> artifact
  - added a non-recursive graph-tailor projection report helper so `/api/graph` can expose tailored node IDs without cycling through graph-scoreboard/research-stress.
- Updated `data/graph-projection-guard.js`:
  - quarantines unresolved relationships and low-confidence draft-only relationships separately
  - reports `unresolvedRelationships`, `draftOnlyQuarantines`, and `inspectionOnlyQuarantines`
  - treats a relationship as covered when it is rendered, quarantined, or both for draft-only safety
- Updated `data/graph-scoreboard.js` normalization so `tailored-narrative` is a first-class modeled entity family.
- Updated `data/graph-depth-score.js` so the normalization repair check passes when graph normalization is already high, instead of only passing in the low-score repair-path case.
- Updated validation and API contracts to require rendered projection relationships, zero unresolved projection relationships, and draft-only-only quarantine after projection nodes are rendered.

Evidence:

- Before:
  - `npm run audit:graph-guard` produced `graph-projection-guard-mppdufey 11/11 check(s) passed; 81 quarantined; 3 family/families; score 100/100; wrote var/graph-projection-guard-receipts.json`

## Next Action

Wave 132: continue strengthening the graph/evaluation spine by either adding deeper public graph controls around manual narrative readiness or moving the next high-value evaluation surface from strong local data into receipt-backed, cross-route, runtime-attested proof.

## Completed Wave 132

Wave 132: split local manual review readiness from external-use readiness for tailored narratives.

Rationale:

- After Wave 131, graph-depth showed a strong overall graph score but a weak manual narrative readiness signal: external-use readiness was `16/100` and all 3 audiences were blocked.
- That block is correct because drafts should not be sent, submitted, published, or used with private material. The missing product capability was a separate local-review gate: the app should be able to say a draft is safe to inspect and repair locally without implying it is safe for external use.

Changes:

- Updated `data/narrative-tailor.js`:
  - added `localReviewReady`, `localReviewScore`, and `localReviewBoundary` to each `manualReadinessGate`
  - added `averageLocalReviewReadinessScore`, `localReviewReady`, and `localReviewBlocked` to report summary
  - kept `readyForManualExternalUse` and `manualReadinessBlocked` unchanged, so external-use safety remains conservative
  - expanded manual-readiness checks to require local-review fields
- Updated `data/graph-depth-score.js`:
  - added local-review readiness to graph-depth summary
  - folded local-review readiness into the manual narrative gate lane while still surfacing external-use blocked status
  - updated the manual gate depth summary text to distinguish local review from send-ready copy
- Updated `server.js` terminal output for `narrative-tailor` so the command reports both external readiness and local review score.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` to enforce the local-review contract.

## Completed Wave 133

Wave 133: add a receipt-backed artifact gap workbench for screenshot/proof repair.

Rationale:

- Manual narrative readiness blockers repeatedly pointed at screenshot/proof gaps, but the existing artifact catalog/replay surfaces did not provide a focused, receipt-backed triage queue tied to those blockers.
- This wave adds an explicit workbench that ranks gaps by narrative impact while preserving the hard safety rule: generated previews, replays, transcripts, and closure plans are not screenshots.

Changes:

- Added `data/artifact-gap-workbench.js`:
  - public-safe artifact gap report
  - plan/history/receipt helpers
  - narrative-blocker tracing from tailored narrative gates
  - closure artifact and replay dependency checks
  - acceptance criteria and forbidden-claim guards for every gap
- Added `scripts/artifact-gaps.mjs` recorder.
- Added API routes in `server.js`:
  - `/api/artifact-gaps`
  - `/api/artifact-gaps/plan`
  - `/api/artifact-gaps/history`
- Added terminal command `artifact-gaps`.
- Added route manifest and refresh-plan coverage.
- Added `audit:artifact-gaps` and syntax checks to `package.json`.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` with artifact-gap workbench contracts.

## Next Action

Wave 134: continue the proof-gap repair campaign by making the artifact gap workbench feed another surface, likely opportunity derisking or narrative repair queues, so screenshot/proof blockers are not just visible but routed into concrete next-action planning.

## Completed Wave 134

Wave 134: route artifact-gap workbench priorities into a proof repair planning surface linked to opportunity derisking.

Rationale:

- The artifact gap workbench had a ranked repair queue and knew which audiences each gap blocked, but that information wasn't connected to the opportunity derisking plans that also block those audiences.
- Wave 134 adds a new `artifact-gap-proof-repair` surface that maps each gap to the opportunity packages it would unblock, generates a 4-step repair path, and writes a receipt so the link is audit-able.

Changes:

- Added `data/artifact-gap-proof-repair.js`: new module that takes `gapWorkbench + opportunityPackages + deRisking` and produces a prioritized proof repair queue. Each item carries: `blockedAudiences`, `linkedOpportunityIds`, `linkedDeRiskPlanIds`, `unlockScore`, a 4-step `repairPath`, `forbiddenClaims` guards, and a verification command. Receipt persistence at `var/artifact-gap-repair-receipts.json`.
- Added `scripts/artifact-gap-repair.mjs`: recorder that spins up a temporary server, fetches `/api/artifact-gap-repair`, and writes a receipt.
- Updated `server.js`: added `require` for the new module, `currentArtifactGapProofRepairQueue()` function, three new routes (`/api/artifact-gap-repair`, `/api/artifact-gap-repair/plan`, `/api/artifact-gap-repair/history`), and a `artifact-gap-repair` / `gap-repair` / `proof-repair` terminal command.
- Updated `data/runtime-attestation.js`: added the 3 new routes to `publicApiRoutes`.
- Updated `package.json`: added `"repair:proof-gaps"` script and `--check` entries for both new files.

Verification:

  - `npm run check` passed: all syntax checks and `scripts/validate-data.mjs`.
  - `npm run repair:proof-gaps` result: `7/7 check(s) passed; 13 repair item(s); 2 opportunity unlock(s); 2 de-risk advance(s); audit 100/100`.
  - Live server `/api/evaluation/research-stress`: `proofActionScenarioPassing: true`, score `99/100`, `8/8` scenarios passing.

## Completed Wave 135

Wave 135: make proof-gap repair durable by routing gaps into opportunity derisking and evidence refresh.

Rationale:

- The artifact-gap workbench and proof-repair queue were useful, but opportunity derisking still needed to feel the same proof-media pressure directly.
- The new artifact-gap-repair endpoint also needed refresh-plan coverage so it would remain part of the warmed runtime evidence set, not just the route manifest.

Changes:

- Updated `data/opportunity-derisking.js` so `buildOpportunityDeRiskingReport()` accepts `artifactGapWorkbench` and converts gap priorities into:
  - `artifactGapPressure` on each opportunity plan.
  - local-only `repair-artifact-gap-*` de-risk steps.
  - `artifactGapQueue` top-level work items.
  - summary counters for `artifactGapWorkItems`, high-priority items, narrative blockers, and queue items.
  - new checks `artifact-gap-routing` and `artifact-gap-claim-guards`.
- Updated `server.js` so `/api/opportunity-derisking` and selected de-risking plans pass the current artifact-gap workbench into opportunity derisking.
- Updated `scripts/opportunity-derisking.mjs`, `scripts/validate-data.mjs`, and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` to record and enforce artifact-gap pressure.
- Updated `data/evidence-refresh.js` to include `/api/artifact-gap-repair`.
- Updated `data/artifact-gap-proof-repair.js` to require refresh-plan coverage with a new `refresh-plan` check and `summary.refreshCovered`.
- Updated `server.js`, `scripts/validate-data.mjs`, and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` so artifact-gap repair route, plan, history, terminal command, validation, and refresh coverage are all contract-tested.

## Next Action

Wave 136: make the new artifact-gap repair and opportunity-gap pressure impossible to miss in a daily/operator surface, likely by feeding it into the local private cockpit or private prioritization digest while preserving the no-external-action boundary.

## Completed Wave 136

Wave 136: route artifact-gap proof repair into the gated private priority stack.

Rationale:

- The proof-gap repair queue was now public-safe, refreshed, and tied to opportunity derisking, but an operator still had to know to run a specific proof-gap command.
- The local private prioritization surface is the right daily/operator layer because it already ranks work, blocks external writes, and wraps every item in manual-only execution locks.

Changes:

- Updated `data/private-priorities.js`:
  - Added optional `artifactGapRepair` input.
  - Converts top artifact-gap repair queue entries into `artifact-gap-proof-repair` priority items in the `proof-repair` lane.
  - Adds `summary.artifactGapRepairItems` and `summary.artifactGapRepairPriorities`.
  - Adds `artifact-gap-repair-priorities` check.
  - Ensures at least one artifact-gap repair item survives the 24-item priority cap when a queue is supplied.
- Updated `server.js` so `/api/private/priorities` receives the current artifact-gap proof repair queue.
- Updated `scripts/private-priorities.mjs` receipt output to include artifact-gap repair priority count.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` fixtures/assertions for the private-priority artifact-gap lane.

## Completed Wave 137

Wave 137: project artifact-gap repair pressure into graph-depth/graph-lineage so proof-media gaps are visible as graph relationships, not only queues and priorities.

Rationale:

- Artifact-gap repair had become a queue, a refresh-verified endpoint, an opportunity de-risking input, and a private priority item, but the public graph still did not show that missing proof media was blocking opportunity-ready claims.
- The graph lineage/depth layers should make proof-media repair pressure visible as inspectable relationships: project -> screenshot gap -> repair node -> opportunity proof unlock.
- The projection has to stay public-safe and cycle-free, so it derives lightweight graph repair nodes from screenshot gaps and opportunity packages instead of calling the artifact-gap repair queue inside the graph builder.

Changes:

- Updated `server.js` graph payload generation with `artifact-gap-repair` nodes and three public-safe edge families:
  - `has-artifact-gap-repair`
  - `planned-by-gap-repair`
  - `unblocks-opportunity-proof`
- Added a cycle-safe graph repair projection helper that scores repair nodes by linked opportunity packages and narrative impact without invoking the heavier repair queue.
- Updated `data/graph-lineage.js` with artifact-gap repair lineage extraction, graph-resolved path tracking, summary counters, and the `artifact-gap-repair-lineage` check.
- Updated `data/graph-depth-score.js` with:
  - an `artifact-gap-repair-depth` lane.
  - an `artifact-gap-to-opportunity-repair` depth summary path.
  - `artifact-gap-repair-graph-depth` check coverage.
  - a non-claim clarifying that graph repair paths are not completed screenshots, videos, outreach, applications, or external opportunity outcomes.
- Updated `scripts/graph-lineage.mjs` and `scripts/graph-depth-score.mjs` receipt output to print artifact-gap repair path coverage.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs` fixtures/assertions so validation and API contracts enforce the new public graph lineage/depth relationships.

  - Ran `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions -p ...` for a bounded Wave 137 review.

- `npm run audit:graph-depth` produced `graph-depth-score-mpphikew 13/13 check(s) passed; 8/8 lane(s); 23/23 artifact gap repair path(s); score 99/100; wrote var/graph-depth-score-receipts.json`

Wave 138: make artifact-gap repair graph pressure visible in the actual command-center UI, so a visitor/operator can see proof-media blockers and opportunity unlock paths without needing to know the graph-lineage or graph-depth audit endpoints exist.

- Wave 137 proved repair pressure in the public graph APIs and audit reports, but the visible command center still mostly showed claims, opportunities, and receipts.

## Completed Wave 139

Wave 139: wire artifact-gap repair graph pressure into terminal/audit operator readouts so `graph-lineage`, `graph-depth`, and related command-center commands surface proof-repair path counts directly instead of burying them in JSON or fallback UI.

Rationale:

- Wave 138 made the browser UI show proof repair paths, but terminal operators still had to infer the new repair graph pressure from API JSON.
- The local command surface should report the same proof-repair truth directly, because the command center is meant to be an operator console, not just a static portfolio.

- Updated `server.js` terminal help so `artifact-gap-repair` is listed as a first-class command.

- Updated `graph-depth` terminal output to print artifact-gap proof-repair path counts and promote the `artifact-gap-to-opportunity-repair` depth summary into the visible terminal summary list.
- Updated `test/api-contract.test.mjs` to enforce the new terminal output for `help`, `graph-lineage`, and `graph-depth`.

## Next Action

## Completed Wave 140

Wave 140: feed the graph-visible proof-repair pressure into a self-review/change-history narrative so every future review can explain which proof gaps are blocking public trust, opportunity readiness, and runtime claims in one audit trail.

Rationale:

- Artifact-gap repair pressure was visible in APIs, the graph UI, and terminal commands, but future review artifacts still lacked a durable narrative explaining how proof gaps affect public trust and opportunity readiness.
- The self-review and change-history surfaces are the right durable audit trail because they are already used to summarize recurring work and public-safe metric drift.

Changes:

- Updated `data/self-review.js`:
  - Adds `proofRepairReview` to weekly/monthly self-review reports.
  - Summarizes repair items, high-priority items, opportunity unlocks, de-risk advances, graph repair nodes, graph repair paths, and graph-resolved repair paths.
  - Adds a `proof-repair-graph` next action ahead of ordinary maintenance/opportunity tasks when proof repair pressure exists.
  - Adds an uncertainty boundary that explicitly says proof repair does not claim missing media, outreach, applications, or third-party outcomes exist.
- Updated `data/change-history.js`:
  - Adds proof-repair metrics to public-safe snapshots.
  - Adds `proofRepairNarrative` to change-history reports.
  - Adds summary counters for repair items, graph repair paths, and graph-resolved repair paths.
- Updated `server.js`:
  - Passes artifact-gap repair and graph-lineage reports into self-review and change-history snapshots.
  - Adds proof-repair lines to `review weekly` / `review monthly` terminal output.
  - Adds proof-repair narrative output to the `changes` terminal command.
- Updated `scripts/change-history.mjs` so local snapshot recording captures at least artifact-gap repair item pressure.
- Updated `test/api-contract.test.mjs` to enforce proof-repair review blocks, proof-repair change-history metrics, proof-repair narrative boundaries, and terminal output visibility.

Evidence:

- Targeted syntax checks passed for:
  - `data/self-review.js`
  - `data/change-history.js`

  - `server.js`
  - `test/api-contract.test.mjs`

## Next Action

Wave 141: convert proof-repair pressure into a dedicated public-safe “trust blockade” model that unifies weak claims, missing artifacts, graph repair paths, opportunity blockers, and runtime receipts into one ranked repair frontier.

## Completed Wave 141

Wave 141: convert proof-repair pressure into a dedicated public-safe “trust blockade” model that unifies weak claims, missing artifacts, graph repair paths, opportunity blockers, and runtime receipts into one ranked repair frontier.

Rationale:

- After Waves 137-140, proof repair pressure existed in graph APIs, UI, terminal output, self-review, and change history, but there was still no single ranked frontier explaining what most blocks public trust.
- The app needed a public-safe blockade model that can unify weak claims, missing proof media, graph repair paths, opportunity blockers, runtime receipt staleness, review narratives, and change-history drift without claiming external outcomes.

Changes:

- Added `data/trust-blockade.js`:
  - Builds `public-safe-trust-blockade-frontier`.
  - Ranks blockers across weak claims, artifact-gap repair, graph repair paths, opportunity blockers, runtime receipts, weekly review narrative, and change-history proof-repair drift.
  - Adds checks for frontier depth, family coverage, route manifest coverage, refresh coverage, script coverage, and local/manual boundary.
  - Adds non-claims that explicitly rule out missing media, outreach, applications, publishing, deployments, and third-party outcomes.
- Added `scripts/trust-blockade.mjs` recorder and `npm run audit:trust-blockade`.
- Updated `server.js`:
  - Adds `/api/trust-blockade`, `/api/trust-blockade/plan`, and `/api/trust-blockade/history`.
  - Adds `trust-blockade` / `trust-frontier` / `blockade` terminal command output.
  - Adds `trust-blockade` to terminal help.
- Updated `data/evidence-refresh.js` and `data/runtime-attestation.js` so trust blockade is refresh-covered and route-manifest visible.
- Updated `package.json` check/script coverage for the new data module and recorder.
- Updated `test/api-contract.test.mjs` to enforce route manifest coverage, refresh coverage, API response shape, checks, non-claims, history/plan routes, and terminal command output.

Evidence:

## Completed Wave 142

Wave 142: expose the trust-blockade frontier in the command-center UI and proof ribbon so the ranked blocker model is visible to humans, not only API callers and terminal operators.

Rationale:

- Wave 141 created a ranked trust-blockade frontier, but humans still had to use `/api/trust-blockade` or the terminal command to see it.
- The command center should show the blocker frontier in first-screen proof health and the truth ledger, while keeping core search/guide/terminal startup fast.

Changes:

- Updated `command-center.mjs`:
  - Adds `app.trustBlockade`.
  - Loads `/api/trust-blockade` asynchronously after the core command center renders, guide results load, proof terminal output runs, status refresh completes, and the constellation starts.
  - Re-renders the proof ribbon and truth ledger when the trust-blockade payload arrives.
  - Adds a proof-ribbon `blockade` tile with frontier item count, family count, high-priority blocker count, and `trust-blockade` command action.
  - Adds a truth-ledger `Trust blockade` row with score, blocker count, and graph repair path count.
- Updated `styles.css` so the proof ribbon can handle the fifth proof tile with an auto-fit grid while keeping the existing mobile two-column override.

Wave 143: reduce trust-blockade UI hydration latency by deriving a lightweight first-screen blockade preview from already-loaded graph/opportunity/runtime summaries, then reconciling it with the heavy `/api/trust-blockade` audit when that endpoint finishes.

## Completed Wave 143

Wave 143: reduce trust-blockade UI hydration latency by deriving a lightweight first-screen blockade preview from already-loaded graph/opportunity/runtime summaries, then reconciling it with the heavy `/api/trust-blockade` audit when that endpoint finishes.

Rationale:

- Wave 142 made the trust-blockade frontier visible, but the UI could still wait on the expensive `/api/trust-blockade` route before showing blockade proof health.
- The first-screen command center needs a fast public-safe blocker signal while the full audit remains available as a later reconciliation layer.
- The first E2E rerun proved the deeper issue: the heavy hydration route could monopolize the single local server and delay ordinary `/api/guide` search updates.

Changes:

- Updated `command-center.mjs`:
  - Derives `app.trustBlockade` immediately from already-loaded trust, artifact, graph, opportunity, runtime-surface, and runtime-reconciliation summaries.
  - Adds preview fields for frontier items, families, high-priority blockers, weak claims, screenshot gaps, graph repair paths, opportunity blockers, runtime blockers, and score.
  - Extends `graphRepairSummaryFromGraph()` with resolved repair-path counts so preview math stays graph-grounded.
  - Replaces eager heavy hydration with a quiet-window scheduler that waits for a short no-interaction period before calling `/api/trust-blockade`.
- Updated `tests/e2e/command-center.spec.mjs`:
  - Tightens proof-ribbon and truth-ledger trust-blockade assertions from 30s to 5s.
  - Adds mobile coverage for the trust-blockade proof action.
- Updated `server.js`, `test/api-contract.test.mjs`, and `scripts/validate-data.mjs` so source-signal audits count the new `blockade` ribbon signal and `trust-blockade` proof action as the fifth first-screen proof action.
- Rebuilt the inline runtime in `index.html`.

Evidence:

  - It exited without useful output again, so the implementation continued locally without leaving the helper process running.

Wave 144: split the heaviest public audit endpoints into cached receipt-backed fast paths plus explicit refresh commands, so route crawls, API contracts, runtime-surface probes, and first-screen UI cannot be held hostage by expensive synchronous audit recomputation.

## Completed Wave 144

Wave 144: split the heaviest public audit endpoints into cached receipt-backed fast paths plus explicit refresh commands, so route crawls, API contracts, runtime-surface probes, and first-screen UI cannot be held hostage by expensive synchronous audit recomputation.

Rationale:

- Wave 143 made trust-blockade visible quickly, but the full `/api/trust-blockade` computation still cost roughly 25s when reached by route crawls, evidence refresh, API contracts, or late UI hydration.
- The app needs a distinction between "read the latest verified local receipt fast" and "rerun the expensive audit now."
- The public endpoint should stay truthful: cached by default, explicit about receipt provenance, and still refreshable on demand.

Changes:

- Updated `data/trust-blockade.js`:
  - Adds `buildTrustBlockadeReportFromReceipt()`.
  - Reuses one `trustBlockadeNonClaims()` source for live and cached reports.
  - Reconstructs a public-safe trust-blockade report from the latest local receipt with `cachedFromReceipt`, `cachePolicy`, `refreshEndpoint`, receipt id, checked-at timestamp, non-claims, checks, families, frontier rows, and verification commands.
- Updated `server.js`:
  - `/api/trust-blockade` now prefers the latest receipt-backed fast report when one exists.
  - `/api/trust-blockade?refresh=1` forces the full recomputation path.
  - The terminal `trust-blockade` command uses the receipt-backed fast report when available.
- Updated `scripts/trust-blockade.mjs` so `npm run audit:trust-blockade` calls `/api/trust-blockade?refresh=1` and continues to write a fresh receipt from the full audit.

  - `/api/trust-blockade?refresh=1` returned `200` in `25677ms` with `cachedFromReceipt: false`, score `100`, frontier `12`.
- `npm run audit:trust-blockade` still forced the full route and produced `trust-blockade-mppkpy3r 6/6 check(s) passed; 12 frontier item(s); 6 family/families; 23/23 graph repair path(s); score 100/100; wrote var/trust-blockade-receipts.json`

## Completed Wave 145

Wave 145: add a route-latency heatmap receipt that records per-endpoint and per-terminal-command durations, then use it to convert the next slowest public audit surfaces into receipt-backed fast paths with explicit refresh commands.

Rationale:

- Wave 144 proved receipt-backed fast paths can reduce the cost of heavyweight public audit routes, but the next bottleneck was still guesswork.
- The API contract suite and terminal sweep were spending most of their time in broad public route traversal and terminal command recomputation.
- The app now needs a local latency ledger that ranks routes and commands by measured cost before the next cache/refresh split.

Changes:

- Added `data/route-latency.js`:
  - Defines the route-latency heatmap plan, terminal command corpus, report builder, latest cached endpoint payload, receipt storage, scoring checks, slow frontiers, repair recommendations, non-claims, and next action.
  - Measures only local public API routes and public-safe terminal commands.
  - Preserves POST bodies from runtime-surface probes so `/api/terminal` is measured through the same real route shape.
- Added `scripts/route-latency.mjs`:
  - Starts a temporary local server.
  - Measures every public API route from `runtimeRouteManifest()`.
  - Measures 20 terminal commands through `/api/terminal`.
  - Writes bounded receipts to `var/route-latency-receipts.json`.
- Updated `server.js`:
  - Exposes `/api/route-latency`, `/api/route-latency/plan`, and `/api/route-latency/history`.
  - Adds the `route-latency` terminal command.
  - Adds `recordRouteLatency` to runtime surface critical scripts.
- Updated `data/runtime-attestation.js`, `data/runtime-truth.js`, `data/evidence-refresh.js`, `scripts/evidence-refresh.mjs`, `scripts/validate-data.mjs`, `test/api-contract.test.mjs`, `package.json`, and `README.md` so the new heatmap is part of the runtime manifest, refresh plan, runtime truth endpoint list, validation suite, package scripts, and docs.

- Wave 145 measured `/api/graph-depth-score` at `10637ms` and terminal `graph-depth` at `13439ms`.
- The graph-depth report already had a receipt store and recorder, so it was a strong candidate for the same cached-default/explicit-refresh split used for trust blockade.

## Completed Wave 148

Wave 148: convert `/api/artifact-gap-repair` and terminal `artifact-gap-repair` into receipt-backed fast paths with explicit refresh, because `route-latency-mppndvc7` identifies it as both the slowest verified route and slowest verified terminal command.

Rationale:

- Wave 147 removed `/api/evaluation/sample` from the top route frontier, and `route-latency-mppndvc7` then identified artifact-gap repair as both the slowest route and slowest terminal command.
- Artifact-gap repair already had a durable receipt store and a full recorder path, so the public route could safely default to the latest receipt while preserving an explicit refresh path for recomputation.
- This keeps the operator-facing route fast without pretending the cached receipt is a fresh recomputation.

  - `npm run repair:proof-gaps` now calls `/api/artifact-gap-repair?refresh=1` so the recorder still writes a fresh full-computation receipt.
- Updated `scripts/validate-data.mjs` and `test/api-contract.test.mjs`:
  - Validates live and cached artifact-gap repair report shapes.
  - Asserts the public route exposes the refresh endpoint and cache flag.

## Completed Wave 149

Wave 149: convert `/api/self-review/weekly`, `/api/self-review`, and their terminal surfaces into receipt-backed fast paths with explicit refresh.

Rationale:

- `route-latency-mppnsr4e` identified `/api/self-review/weekly` as the slowest measured route at `8146ms`, with `/api/self-review` also near the top at `6783ms`.
- Self-review had no first-class receipt store yet, so this wave added one instead of hiding the recomputation cost.
- The route now defaults to the latest local receipt when present and preserves `?refresh=1` for full recomputation.
